# Cuttlefish

Cuttlefish are marine molluscs of the order Sepiida belonging to the class Cephalopoda, which also includes squid, octopuses, and nautiluses. Despite their name, cuttlefish are not fish but rather molluscs. Cuttlefish have a unique internal shell, the cuttlebone, which is used for control of buoyancy. They have eight arms and two tentacles furnished with denticulated suckers, with which they secure their prey. They generally range in size from 15 to 25 centimetres (6 to 10 inches), with the largest species, Sepia apama, reaching 50 centimetres (20 inches) in mantle length and over 10.5 kilograms (23 pounds) in mass.

## Etymology

The name "cuttle" derives from the Old English word cudele, possibly cognate with the Old Norse koddi (cushion) and the Middle Low German kudel (rag), in reference to the soft texture of the animal. The Greek-derived term for cuttlefish, sepia, is the source of the English word for the colour, after the rich brown pigment derived from the ink of the cuttlefish. The pigment was once an important commercial product because, before synthetic dyes became widely available in the late nineteenth century, sepia ink was used in writing and drawing.

## Anatomy

Cuttlefish have a flattened, broad, oval body with two long fins running the length of the body. They possess large W-shaped pupils, eight arms, and two tentacles which they use to seize prey. Their tentacles are kept retracted in pouches and shot out at high speed to capture fast-moving prey. Cuttlefish eat small molluscs, crabs, shrimp, fish, octopus, worms, and other cuttlefish. Their predators include dolphins, sharks, fish, seals, seabirds, and other cuttlefish. The typical lifespan of a cuttlefish is approximately one to two years. Studies are said to indicate cuttlefish to be among the most intelligent invertebrates. Cuttlefish also have one of the largest brain-to-body size ratios of all invertebrates.

## Cuttlebone

The cuttlebone, unique to cuttlefish, is an internal structure that consists primarily of aragonite. It has a microscopic, highly porous structure that gives the cuttlefish its buoyancy. The hollow chambers within the cuttlebone fill with gases and liquids as needed to maintain neutral buoyancy. Each species of cuttlefish has a distinct shape, size, and pattern of ridges or texture on the cuttlebone. The cuttlebone, which has a unique shape for each species, is used by experts to identify the species of cuttlefish examined. The cuttlebone is composed of two parts: a dorsal shield and a ventral, chambered structure. The chambered structure is responsible for buoyancy regulation, while the dorsal shield provides protection.

## Coloration

Cuttlefish are sometimes called the "chameleons of the sea" because of their remarkable ability to rapidly alter their skin colour at will. Their skin flashes a fast-changing pattern as communication to other cuttlefish and to camouflage them from predators. This colour-changing function is produced by groups of red, yellow, brown, and black pigmented chromatophores above a layer of reflective iridophores and leucophores, with up to 200 of these specialised pigment cells per square millimetre. The pigmented chromatophores have a sac of pigment and a large membrane that is folded when retracted. Despite their elaborate colour displays, cuttlefish are colour-blind and probably appreciate their displays through a different visual mechanism, perhaps polarisation sensitivity.

## Reproduction

Cuttlefish are reproductively active for the last few months of their relatively short lives. Males are remarkably aggressive when competing for mates. Mature males display zebra-like stripes that signal their intent to fight to other males. The dominant male, normally the larger of the two, will then attempt to intimidate his opponent. If neither flees, the conflict typically becomes physical, lasting for only a few seconds and resulting in only minor wounds. Once a male has chosen his mate, he will use one of his arms, called a hectocotylus, to insert spermatophores into an opening near the female's mouth. The female then deposits eggs—usually 100 to 300 of them—in a hidden area, attached to a substrate. The eggs hatch after a few months, depending on water temperature.

## Diet and predation

Cuttlefish are carnivorous predators. Their diets vary by life stage but typically include small molluscs, crabs, shrimp, fish, octopuses, worms, and other cuttlefish. Hunting strategies are diverse and adaptive. A common technique is to approach prey while displaying a hypnotic, pulsating colour pattern across the skin; researchers hypothesize that this serves to disorient or distract prey. Once within striking range, the two long tentacles shoot forward and capture the prey, which is then drawn back to the eight arms and beak. Their predators in turn include larger fish, sharks, seals, marine mammals, seabirds, and other cuttlefish.

## Intelligence

Cuttlefish are widely regarded as among the most intelligent invertebrates. They are capable of complex behaviours, including problem-solving, observational learning, and the deferred gratification associated with cognition more typical of vertebrate species. In one well-known experiment, cuttlefish were able to wait for a preferred food item rather than immediately consume a less-preferred one, suggesting that they can plan for future rewards in a manner reminiscent of certain primate experiments. The proximate neural basis of cuttlefish cognition is thought to lie in their unusually large and elaborate vertical lobe, which has structural parallels to the vertebrate hippocampus.

## Distribution

Cuttlefish are found in tropical and temperate ocean waters. They are mostly shallow-water animals, although they are known to inhabit depths of about 600 metres. They have an unusual biogeographic pattern: cuttlefish are present along the coasts of East and South Asia, Western Europe, the Mediterranean, as well as all coasts of Africa and Australia. Notably, they are absent from the Americas. The reasons for this absence are not fully understood, but the leading hypothesis involves historical sea-level changes and continental drift cutting off ancestral cuttlefish populations from the eastern Pacific and Atlantic shelf habitats they would otherwise occupy.

## Cuttlefish ink

Like other cephalopods, cuttlefish have ink, used as a defensive measure. Cuttlefish ink was historically an important dye, called sepia. Today, artificial dyes have largely replaced natural sepia, but it is still used in some artistic applications. The ink is produced and stored in an ink sac that connects to the gut just before the anus, allowing the ink to be expelled rapidly through the funnel. Mixed with mucus secreted from a separate gland, the ink forms thick, dark clouds in water that can confuse predators and obscure the cuttlefish's escape. Cuttlefish ink also contains compounds that may directly impair the chemoreception of certain predators, adding a chemical defence to the visual one.

## Use as food

Cuttlefish are caught for food in many countries. The cuttlefish is a popular food source in some Asian, Mediterranean, and European cuisines. Cuttlefish ink is used in some Mediterranean dishes such as black risotto and black pasta. The ink lends a strong, briny flavour and a striking dark colour to the dish. The flesh of the cuttlefish is similar to that of squid and octopus, often prepared by grilling, frying, or stewing. Particular care is taken to remove the cuttlebone and the ink sac before cooking, although in some preparations the ink is intentionally retained.

## Conservation status

Most species of cuttlefish are not currently considered threatened. However, certain populations have shown declines due to overfishing and habitat loss in coastal areas. The giant Australian cuttlefish, Sepia apama, has been the subject of particular concern: a major aggregation in the upper Spencer Gulf, South Australia, exhibited a marked population decline in the early 2010s before partially recovering later in the decade. Conservation measures for this aggregation include seasonal fishing closures and habitat protection of the rocky reefs used for spawning.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. ## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. The neural correlates of these behaviours remain an active area of investigation, particularly given that cephalopod nervous systems evolved independently from those of vertebrates and yet support comparably complex cognition. Comparative studies between cuttlefish and octopuses are particularly useful, since the two groups share a common cephalopod ancestor but have diverged significantly in body plan and lifestyle. Cuttlefish, with their more conservative body plan and reliance on the cuttlebone for buoyancy, occupy a different ecological niche from the more flexible, often benthic octopuses, and the resulting behavioural and cognitive contrasts illuminate which features are general to cephalopods and which are species-specific adaptations.

## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles. ## Additional notes

The study of cuttlefish behaviour has expanded significantly since the early 2000s, driven in large part by improvements in underwater video technology and by laboratory facilities capable of housing cephalopods for extended observation. Researchers have documented a wide range of previously unappreciated behaviours, from cooperative hunting in some species to apparent play behaviour in juveniles.
